note: Each mock points to a difficulty 4-5 scenario; scenarios are seeded first by WritingV2ContentSeeder, then mocks. is_diagnostic=false on these scenarios.

scenarios.title: Mock: complex polypharmacy referral to geriatrician
scenarios.case_notes_markdown:
**Patient:** Mr Albert Trafford  
**DOB:** 04/07/1942 (aged 83)  
**Occupation:** Retired bus driver  
**Today's date:** 26/05/2026

**Reason for today's visit:**  
Follow-up of recurrent unwitnessed falls. Three falls in the past 12 weeks, most recent yesterday at home.

**Subjective:**
- 3 falls in 12 weeks; no head injury, no fracture
- Yesterday's fall: tripped getting out of bed at 02:00; lay on floor for 90 minutes before pendant alarm activated
- Reports increasing unsteadiness on standing
- Lightheaded mornings; no syncope
- Denies chest pain, palpitations, weakness or focal neurology
- Recent weight loss 4 kg in 3 months
- Mood low; appetite poor; sleeping 4-5 hours per night

**Objective:**
- Postural BP: lying 138/82, standing (1 min) 102/64 → 36 mmHg systolic drop
- Pulse 76 regular
- Mini-MOCA 22/30 (mild cognitive impairment)
- Gait: cautious, shortened stride, no broad base
- Romberg negative
- Cardiovascular and respiratory exam unremarkable
- ECG: sinus rhythm; QTc 460

**Investigations to date:**
- FBC, U&E, CRP, TFT, B12, folate, glucose, calcium — all normal
- Urinalysis: leucocytes negative

**Current medications (10):**
- Amlodipine 10 mg daily
- Doxazosin 4 mg daily
- Bendroflumethiazide 2.5 mg daily
- Atorvastatin 40 mg nocte
- Aspirin 75 mg daily
- Citalopram 20 mg daily
- Zopiclone 7.5 mg nocte
- Omeprazole 20 mg daily
- Adcal-D3 BD
- Senna 7.5 mg nocte PRN

**Past medical history:**
- Hypertension (since 2008)
- Depression (since 2015)
- Chronic insomnia (zopiclone for 7 years)
- Osteoporosis (T-score -2.8)
- Mild cognitive impairment (since 2024)

**Social history:**
- Lives alone in two-bedroom bungalow
- Daughter visits weekly
- Pendant alarm worn intermittently
- No carer support

**Allergies:** Nil known.

**Plan from today:**
- Refer to geriatrician for comprehensive geriatric assessment + medication review
- Continue pendant alarm; reinforce wearing
- Bone-health review

**Writing task:** Write a routine referral to Dr Margaret Hewson, Consultant Geriatrician, City General Hospital. Word count 180-200.

scenarios.title: Mock: discharge after complex inpatient stroke recovery
scenarios.case_notes_markdown:
**Patient:** Mrs Patricia Edwards  
**DOB:** 18/09/1953 (aged 72)  
**Admission date:** 02/05/2026  
**Discharge date:** 26/05/2026 (today)

**Reason for admission:**  
Acute left middle cerebral artery territory ischaemic stroke. Presented with right-sided hemiparesis, expressive dysphasia and right facial droop within 90 minutes of onset.

**Initial management:**
- Thrombolysed with alteplase 110 minutes after symptom onset (door-to-needle 38 minutes)
- Admitted to hyperacute stroke unit
- Initial NIHSS 12
- CT head: hyperdense MCA sign; subsequent MRI confirmed infarct
- Carotid Doppler: 60% stenosis left ICA
- Echocardiogram: no thrombus, normal LV function
- Holter 24-hour: paroxysmal atrial fibrillation detected

**Inpatient course:**
- Days 1-7: hyperacute stroke unit; aspirin 300 mg daily for 14 days
- Day 8 transferred to stroke rehabilitation ward
- Daily multidisciplinary therapy (physiotherapy, occupational therapy, speech and language therapy)
- Apixaban 5 mg twice daily commenced day 15 for paroxysmal AF
- Atorvastatin 80 mg nocte commenced
- Antihypertensive optimisation: amlodipine 10 mg daily, ramipril 10 mg daily

**Functional progress:**
- NIHSS reduced from 12 to 3
- Mobile with elbow crutches; independent transfers
- Stair assessment passed with handrail
- Right upper limb: improved from grade 2 to grade 4 power
- Dysphasia mostly resolved; occasional word-finding difficulties remain
- Continent of urine and faeces
- Tolerating normal diet and fluids

**Discharge medications:**
- Apixaban 5 mg twice daily
- Atorvastatin 80 mg nocte
- Amlodipine 10 mg daily
- Ramipril 10 mg daily
- Lansoprazole 30 mg daily

**Past medical history:**
- Hypertension (since 2010)
- Type 2 diabetes mellitus, on diet only, HbA1c 47
- Hypercholesterolaemia

**Allergies:** Nil known.

**Discharge planning:**
- Lives with husband in two-storey house; stairlift fitted during admission
- Community physiotherapy referral arranged (4 weeks)
- Community speech and language therapy referral arranged
- Stroke clinic follow-up in 6 weeks (Dr Adekunle)
- Carotid endarterectomy clinic referral made (target within 14 days)
- DVLA notification advised

**Writing task:** Write a discharge letter to Dr Imran Khan at The Meadows Surgery. Word count 180-200.

scenarios.title: Mock: pharmacy review of new oncology regimen
scenarios.case_notes_markdown:
**Patient:** Mr Henry Garrison  
**DOB:** 12/05/1957 (aged 68)  
**Today's date:** 26/05/2026  
**Setting:** Oncology day unit pharmacist review.

**Reason for letter:**  
Review of newly initiated capecitabine regimen for adjuvant treatment of stage III colorectal carcinoma. Concerns identified at first 21-day cycle review; communication to GP regarding interim management and monitoring.

**Background:**
- Colorectal cancer diagnosed February 2026
- Hemicolectomy March 2026; stage III (T3 N1 M0)
- Commenced adjuvant capecitabine 1250 mg/m2 twice daily for 14 days followed by 7-day rest, 8 cycles planned
- Cycle 1 completed today; cycle 2 due to start 02/06/2026

**Cycle 1 toxicity:**
- Grade 2 hand-foot syndrome (palmar-plantar erythrodysaesthesia) — painful peeling skin on palms and soles
- Grade 2 diarrhoea (5-6 loose stools per day during week 2)
- Mild stomatitis
- Fatigue grade 1
- No nausea or vomiting (well-controlled with metoclopramide)

**Investigations:**
- FBC: WCC 4.2, neutrophils 2.1, Hb 119, platelets 168 (mild anaemia)
- U&E: creatinine 110, eGFR 65 (CKD 2)
- LFTs: ALT 42, ALP 110 (mildly raised baseline)
- DPYD genotyping: heterozygous *2A variant (partial DPD deficiency)

**Current medications:**
- Capecitabine 1250 mg/m2 BD (cycle 1 complete; dose-reduction needed for cycle 2 per DPYD)
- Metoclopramide 10 mg TDS PRN nausea
- Loperamide 4 mg PRN diarrhoea
- Topical urea 10% cream for hand-foot syndrome
- Ramipril 5 mg daily (hypertension)
- Atorvastatin 20 mg nocte
- Lansoprazole 30 mg daily

**Allergies:** Nil known.

**Pharmacy actions taken today:**
- Capecitabine cycle 2 dose recalculated and reduced to 75% (per DPYD heterozygous status and grade 2 toxicity)
- Patient counselled on continued anti-diarrhoeal and skin care
- Hand-foot syndrome severity-grading review booked for day 7 of cycle 2
- Sick-day rules reinforced (stop capecitabine if diarrhoea >4 stools above baseline, fever >38°C, or severe stomatitis)

**Action requested from GP:**
- Awareness of dose reduction and DPYD genotype for future prescribing
- Repeat U&E and FBC in 1 week
- Consideration of formal stoma nurse referral if diarrhoea persists despite loperamide
- Continuation of existing antihypertensives without change

**Writing task:** Write a letter to Mr Garrison's GP, Dr Helena Park at Riverside Medical Centre, summarising the cycle 1 review and outstanding actions. Word count 180-200.

scenarios.title: Mock: pharmacy response to GP query on antibiotic stewardship
scenarios.case_notes_markdown:
**Patient:** Mrs Eleanor Pickering  
**DOB:** 22/11/1947 (aged 78)  
**Today's date:** 26/05/2026  
**Setting:** Antimicrobial pharmacist clinic.

**Reason for letter:**  
Response to Dr Helena Park (GP) regarding her enquiry of 20 May 2026 about Mrs Pickering's recurrent urinary tract infections and possible nitrofurantoin prophylaxis. Mrs Pickering attended antimicrobial pharmacy clinic today.

**Background from GP letter (received 20/05/2026):**
- 6 culture-positive UTIs in past 12 months
- Most recent: E. coli sensitive to nitrofurantoin (last cultured 28/04/2026)
- Previous regimens: trimethoprim (resistance developed), cefalexin, fosfomycin single-dose
- GP requested antimicrobial advice on prophylaxis vs alternative approaches

**Today's clinic findings:**
- Mrs Pickering reports symptoms (dysuria, urgency) typical at intervals of 6-8 weeks
- Maintains good fluid intake (1.5-2 L per day)
- Uses vaginal oestrogen pessary intermittently (not adherent)
- Has tried D-mannose without clear benefit
- No structural urinary tract abnormality on ultrasound (March 2026)
- Renal function: creatinine 105, eGFR 52 — borderline for nitrofurantoin caution

**Pharmacy assessment and plan:**
- Nitrofurantoin 50 mg nocte for 6 months trial proposed — caution with eGFR 52 (above 45 cut-off but borderline; monitor for pulmonary or hepatic toxicity)
- Reinforce daily vaginal oestrogen (proven preventive evidence)
- Continue fluid optimisation
- Stand-by 3-day course of nitrofurantoin if breakthrough symptoms
- Review at 3 months and 6 months in antimicrobial pharmacy clinic

**Past medical history:**
- Recurrent UTI (since 2024)
- Mild chronic kidney disease (eGFR 52)
- Type 2 diabetes mellitus, on metformin 1 g BD
- Hypertension, on ramipril 10 mg daily
- Postmenopausal

**Allergies:** Penicillin (rash).

**Action requested from GP:**
- Prescribe nitrofurantoin 50 mg nocte for 6 months (issued from clinic today as 1-month supply with GP repeat thereafter)
- Annual repeat U&E
- Reinforce vaginal oestrogen adherence at next appointment
- Joint care: antimicrobial pharmacy will provide 3-month and 6-month review and report

**Writing task:** Write a response letter to Dr Helena Park at Riverside Medical Centre. Word count 180-200.

scenarios.title: Mock: complex post-operative discharge with multiple care needs
scenarios.case_notes_markdown:
**Patient:** Mr Gregory Lin  
**DOB:** 11/02/1959 (aged 67)  
**Admission date:** 12/05/2026  
**Discharge date:** 26/05/2026 (today)

**Reason for admission:**  
Elective abdominoperineal resection with end colostomy formation for stage II rectal adenocarcinoma.

**Operative details:**
- APR with end colostomy performed 14/05/2026 by Mr Ashworth (consultant colorectal surgeon)
- General anaesthetic with epidural
- No intra-operative complications; estimated blood loss 350 mL
- Stoma sited in left lower quadrant (pre-marked by stoma nurse)

**Inpatient course:**
- Day 1 post-op: epidural managed pain effectively; mobilised to chair with two-person assist
- Day 3: epidural removed; oral analgesia commenced (paracetamol regular plus codeine)
- Day 5: tolerated diet; bowels working into stoma; passing flatus
- Day 7: independent stoma changes (with prompts); stoma nurse confident with discharge
- Day 10: catheter removed; voiding well; no significant residual
- Day 14: today; medically and surgically fit for discharge

**Stoma assessment at discharge:**
- Stoma: pink, healthy, no signs of retraction or prolapse
- Output: 800-1000 mL formed stool daily
- Skin: peristomal skin intact; one-piece appliance fitted; ordered 3-month supply
- Patient: independent with stoma changes; one teaching session with daughter completed

**Discharge medications:**
- Paracetamol 1 g QDS regular for 4 weeks
- Codeine phosphate 30 mg QDS PRN
- Loperamide 2 mg PRN if output > 1500 mL/day
- Tinzaparin 4500 units SC daily for 28 days from surgery date (extended VTE prophylaxis post pelvic surgery)
- Lansoprazole 30 mg daily
- Ramipril 5 mg daily (continued)

**Past medical history:**
- Hypertension, on ramipril 5 mg daily
- Mild osteoarthritis (right knee)
- No known cognitive impairment
- Ex-smoker (stopped 10 years ago)

**Allergies:** Iodine (rash).

**Discharge home circumstances:**
- Lives with wife (fit and well) in two-storey house
- Bathroom and toilet on first floor
- Stairlift fitted during admission
- Wife working part-time; daughter visits twice weekly

**Required home care:**
- Daily tinzaparin SC injections by district nurse for remaining 14 days
- Stoma nurse community follow-up: home visit day 7 post-discharge, then 2-weekly for 6 weeks
- District nurse to check perineal wound on days 3, 7 and 10 post-discharge
- Surgical follow-up: colorectal clinic 6 weeks post-op (Mr Ashworth)
- Oncology MDT referral made; treatment plan to follow

**Writing task:** Write a discharge letter to the community nursing team at North Riverside Community Hub. Word count 180-200.

scenarios.title: Mock: transfer of complex dementia patient with safeguarding concerns
scenarios.case_notes_markdown:
**Patient:** Mrs Phyllis Cromwell  
**DOB:** 15/08/1937 (aged 88)  
**Transfer date:** 26/05/2026 (today)  
**Setting:** Acute geriatric ward → specialist dementia unit (residential).

**Reason for transfer:**  
Mrs Cromwell is medically fit for discharge but cannot return to her previous home placement due to escalating dementia-related care needs and identified safeguarding concerns. Transfer to Willowbrook Specialist Dementia Unit for ongoing residential care.

**Admission context:**
- Admitted 09/05/2026 following fall at home with hip pain (no fracture on X-ray)
- Found by neighbour after 36 hours on the floor
- On admission: dehydrated (urea 12), grade 2 sacral pressure ulcer, delirium superimposed on dementia
- Adult safeguarding referral made on day 1 of admission due to evidence of neglect at home (soiled clothing, unwashed for several days, empty fridge)

**Inpatient progress:**
- Delirium has resolved with rehydration, infection treatment and environmental optimisation
- Sacral pressure ulcer reduced from grade 2 to grade 1; healing well with daily dressings
- Mobile with two-person assistance for transfers; uses Zimmer frame for short walks
- Cognitive assessment (during stable period): AMTS 4/10, severe dementia consistent with Alzheimer-type
- Requires full assistance with personal care
- Eating and drinking with prompting; thickened fluids per SALT recommendation
- Continent of urine with prompting; occasional faecal incontinence

**Behavioural and psychiatric features:**
- Sundowning: increased agitation late afternoon
- Repetitive questioning (calls for late husband)
- Resistive to personal care at times; needs gentle explanation and choice
- Sleep: requires presence of staff to settle; occasional night-time wandering

**Medications:**
- Donepezil 10 mg nocte
- Quetiapine 25 mg nocte (commenced for severe agitation; under review for tapering)
- Lactulose 15 mL BD
- Adcal-D3 BD
- Paracetamol 1 g QDS regular

**Past medical history:**
- Alzheimer-type dementia (diagnosed 2020; severe)
- Hypertension
- Osteoporosis
- Mild congestive cardiac failure (compensated)

**Allergies:** Penicillin (anaphylaxis).

**Safeguarding outcome:**
- Best interests meeting held 22/05/2026 with multidisciplinary team, social worker and Mrs Cromwell's only relative (great-niece, geographically distant)
- Outcome: placement in specialist dementia unit deemed least restrictive option
- Deprivation of Liberty Safeguards (DoLS) authorisation in process

**Action requested from receiving unit:**
- Continue current medication regimen with quetiapine taper plan
- Continue sacral wound care: daily dressing review with hydrocolloid dressing
- Maintain SALT-recommended thickened fluid regimen
- Implement gentle person-centred care with consistent staff team
- Liaise with allocated social worker (Ms Aisha Kowalski) and great-niece
- Continue DoLS process; authorisation pending

**Writing task:** Write a transfer letter to the senior nurse at Willowbrook Specialist Dementia Unit. Word count 180-200.
